Screen 15 — Slides 31–32 Interactive screen (Student Action)
Feedback flow: slides 31 (happy/unhappy paths)
Slide 31
Slide 32
↑ Should fix
Sources: D2, X1
INSERT after slide 31: Add a Match Pairs drag-to-classify screen (new screen between current screens 14 and 15). Present three quantity-description cards: '3 apples on the plate', 'apples in Candra's sack (a)', 'oranges in Candra's father's sack'. Two target columns: 'Fixed quantity — we know the exact number' and 'Changing quantity — we use a letter'. No guiding prompts. Correct placements earn a tick; incorrect placements return the card with a brief prompt ('Can we count this exactly?'). This is the blueprint's Stage 5 (Apply) interaction and raises the ceiling from guided application to genuine independent classification, unlocking transfer readiness for the parent module's Math_Vault formalisation.
↑ Should fix
Sources: D6, D7
EDIT: On slide 32 (summary screen), add an explicit forward pointer after the 'variables' definition: 'In the next activity, we will learn the proper mathematical names for these ideas.' This bridges the applet's concrete-phase language ('known/unknown quantities') to the module's Math_Vault formalisation of 'variable' and 'constant', as specified in the blueprint's Stage 6 Consolidate convention requirement. Also add a 2–3 second pause or a brief reflection prompt ('Think: what does the letter a tell us about the apples in the sack?') before the 'Start Over' button appears, giving the student a moment to consolidate before the applet ends.
↑ Should fix
Sources: L5
EDIT: Add dev notes specifying that the 'Start Over' button must be minimum 44×44px tap area per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.5. Also confirm that the button resets the applet to slide 2 (title screen).
StageStage 6 — Consolidate Verify tap independent Concrete
Concept
distinguish a visible, countable quantity (3 apples on a plate) from a hidden, uncountable quantity (apples in a sack) using the everyday labels 'known quantity' and 'unknown quantity'
recognise that a letter can stand in for the count of an unknown quantity — specifically that 'a' means HOW MANY apples are in the sack, not the name of the fruit
identify the correct variable label for a given unknown quantity from a three-option MCQ when the label is already displayed on the sack image
select a different letter for a second unknown quantity when the first letter is already assigned
state that different unknown quantities require different letters. These fluencies are concrete-phase and pre-notational — no algebraic form, no 3n notation, and no formal definition of 'variable' as
Vocab in screenreinforced fixed quantity reinforced changing quantity
Misc addressedM252 eff 3/3
M252 eff 3/3
Letter as fruit-name abbreviation — surface form of M252 (a = apple the fruit, not how many apples)
how: Slides 19–22: 'Remember — x/y/z/a is THE NUMBER of apples, not the fruit itself' callout on each animation frame; Slide 30: 'Oops! a is already the number of apples! We don't know that there are the s
Visual modelVIR Full Summary Two-Column Table with Exit Question Panel — novel, no KB VIR Concrete
ConventionFixed quantity vs. constant — vocabulary sequencing
Fixed quantity vs. constant — vocabulary sequencing
This applet operates at the concrete phase and must use only the everyday labels 'fixed quantity' and 'changing quantity' in all student-facing text. The formal terms 'constant' and 'variable' must NO
 Letter choice: 'a' for apples, 'b' for oranges — arbitrary letter principle
Letter choice: 'a' for apples, 'b' for oranges — arbitrary letter principle
When 'b' is introduced for oranges in Stage 5 (Apply) and Stage 6 (Consolidate), student-facing text must explicitly state that any letter (not just 'b') could be used — the choice is arbitrary — and
Shout_Out_Loud sequencing — fires after classification is secure
Shout_Out_Loud sequencing — fires after classification is secure
The Shout_Out_Loud choral-response prompt ('Say it together: a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — not the name of the fruit!') must appear at Stage 6 (Consolidate), AFTER the fixed/changing cl
After completing this applet, students will understand that some quantities are visible and countable (known quantities, written as numbers) while others are hidden and uncountable (unknown quantities, represented by letters). They will recognise that a letter like 'a' stands for HOW MANY of an object, not the name of the object itself, and that different unknown quantities require different letters. These fluencies are concrete-phase and pre-notational — students have not yet encountered algebraic form (3n), formal definitions of 'variable' and 'constant', or independent transfer to novel scenarios.
Mastery Assessment 72% coverage
Coverage 72%
The applet covers its scoped LO well at the concrete observation and guided-application levels. The core concept — a letter labels HOW MANY, not the object's name — is introduced, animated, and practised with feedback. The 'HOW MANY' callout appears four times. The different-letters-for-different-unknowns rule is introduced and practised with a targeted distractor. Coverage is reduced from full because: (1) the Commit-Before-Reveal classification interaction (blueprint Stage 3) was not implemented — students never independently classified a quantity as fixed or changing before the answer was revealed; (2) the M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
 canonical distractor ('a stands for apple the fruit') was not presented as a dedicated MCQ option, so the Counterexample Feedback that is the applet's most critical instructional moment was never triggered; (3) the Match Pairs independent application stage (blueprint Stage 5) was absent, leaving the ceiling at guided application; (4) the thought-bubble animation showing multiple possible counts (5? 10? 20?) was absent, weakening the 'a could be any number' principle. These are student-experience gaps, not blueprint-element absences.
What students CAN do after this module
After completing this applet as reviewed, students can: (1) distinguish a visible, countable quantity (3 apples on a plate) from a hidden, uncountable quantity (apples in a sack) using the everyday labels 'known quantity' and 'unknown quantity'; (2) recognise that a letter can stand in for the count of an unknown quantity — specifically that 'a' means HOW MANY apples are in the sack, not the name of the fruit; (3) identify the correct variable label for a given unknown quantity from a three-option MCQ when the label is already displayed on the sack image; (4) select a different letter for a second unknown quantity when the first letter is already assigned; (5) state that different unknown quantities require different letters. These fluencies are concrete-phase and pre-notational — no algebraic form, no 3n notation, and no formal definition of 'variable' as a stable, independently recalled term.
Fluency gaps — what students CANNOT reliably do
Students cannot yet: (1) independently classify a quantity as fixed or changing without the label already visible on screen — the applet never required a Commit-Before-Reveal classification before the answer was shown, so the perceptual distinction is observed but not independently exercised; (2) apply the 'HOW MANY' principle to a novel context without scaffolding — the M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
 concrete-phase distractor ('a stands for apple the fruit') was not presented as a dedicated MCQ option, so the canonical counterexample was never triggered; (3) explain why any letter (not just 'a') could represent the unknown — the arbitrary-letter principle was demonstrated via animation but never independently applied in a drag-or-classify interaction; (4) transfer the fixed/changing distinction to a new scenario without the fruit-sack visual anchor — the Match Pairs independent application stage was absent, leaving the ceiling at guided application only; (5) recall the formal term 'variable' reliably — 'VARIABLES' was introduced mid-sequence on slide 23 (Stage 4, before the permitted Stage 6 summary location), creating a vocabulary-sequencing violation that may cause confusion about when and how the term applies. The unaddressed M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
 concrete-phase surface (letter as fruit-name abbreviation) remains a live risk for all subsequent applets and module slides that introduce algebraic notation.
Conceptual depth
3/5 — Students understand WHY a letter is needed (the sack quantity cannot be counted, so a symbol stands in for the count) before HOW it is written. The 'HOW MANY, not the name' principle is explicitly stated four times across slides 19–22 and reinforced in the summary. However, the WHY anchor is delivered primarily through passive animation (the letter-cycling sequence) rather than through a student commitment, and the deepest WHY — that the letter could represent any number (5? 10? 20?) — is absent because the thought-bubble animation was not implemented. The concept is grounded but not yet generative.
Transfer readiness
2/5 — Students can apply the fixed/changing distinction within the Candra fruit-sack scenario but have not been asked to transfer it to a novel context. The Match Pairs independent application stage (blueprint Stage 5) was not implemented, so the only independent practice was a guided MCQ where the correct answer was visually displayed on the sack. Students are not yet ready to encounter a new concrete scenario (e.g., 'Budi has some marbles') and independently assign a variable letter without scaffolding.
Mastery path to 100
Students have never independently classified a quantity as fixed or changing — every classification was either passive observation or followed an already-visible label. The perceptual distinction is seen but not exercised.
Unlocks genuine independent discrimination of fixed vs. changing quantities — the foundational perceptual skill that all subsequent algebraic variable work depends on. Without this, students have observed the distinction but never owned it.
Insert a Commit-Before-Reveal screen after the side-by-side contrast is established (between slides 15 and 17): present both the plate and the sack with two tap-buttons beneath each ('Fixed quantity' / 'Changing quantity') and require the student to classify both before the labels are revealed. Add targeted corrective feedback for each wrong tap.
The M252 canonical distractor — 'a stands for apple (the fruit)' — was never presented as a selectable option. The Counterexample Feedback ('We already know the fruit is called an apple — a tells us HOW MANY') was never triggered. Students have seen the 'HOW MANY' callout passively but have never been forced to reject the abbreviation interpretation under their own choice.
Directly surfaces and corrects the concrete-phase form of M252. Without this, students who hold the abbreviation interpretation will pass through the applet without ever having their misconception challenged. All subsequent module slides that introduce algebraic notation (3n, 1 + 3n) will be built on an unchallenged false belief.
On screen 13 (slides 24–27), add a fourth MCQ option: 'a — because a stands for apple (the fruit)'. When selected, fire Counterexample Feedback: 'We already know the fruit is called an apple! The letter a does not name the fruit — a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the applet's single most important instructional moment per the blueprint and research brief.
Students have no independent transfer task — the only practice is a guided MCQ where the correct variable label ('a') is already displayed on the sack image, making the correct answer visually obvious. Students have never applied the fixed/changing distinction to a new set of quantities without scaffolding.
Raises the ceiling from guided application to genuine independent classification. Unlocks transfer readiness for the parent module's Math_Vault formalisation of 'variable' and 'constant', and for sibling applets G7C4M1A3 and G7C4M1A4 which build on this concrete foundation.
After screen 14 (oranges variable selection), insert a Match Pairs drag-to-classify screen: present three quantity-description cards ('3 apples on the plate', 'apples in Candra's sack (a)', 'oranges in Candra's father's sack') and two target columns ('Fixed quantity — we know the exact number' / 'Changing quantity — we use a letter'). No guiding prompts. Correct placements earn a tick; incorrect placements return the card with a brief prompt ('Can we count this exactly?').
The 'a could be any number' principle — that the variable represents a quantity that could be 5, or 10, or 20, not a fixed value — was not demonstrated. The thought-bubble animation showing multiple possible counts was absent. Students see 'a' as a label for an unknown count but may not yet understand that it represents a range of possible values.
Closes the gap between 'a labels an unknown' and 'a represents any possible value' — the conceptual distinction that separates the concrete-phase variable-as-label understanding from the deeper M252 misconception (variable as mystery unknown to solve for). Without this, students may still treat 'a' as a specific hidden number.
At the moment 'a' is introduced on slide 22/23, add a brief thought-bubble animation on the sack showing '5? 10? 20?' cycling inside the sack before settling on 'a apples'. This reinforces that 'a' is not a specific hidden number waiting to be found — it could be any number.
The formal term 'VARIABLES' was introduced mid-sequence on slide 23 (Stage 4, Explain) rather than reserved for the Stage 6 (Consolidate) summary panel as the blueprint and research brief require. This front-loads the formal vocabulary before the concrete distinction is secure and creates a vocabulary-sequencing inconsistency.
Restores the correct concrete-phase vocabulary sequencing. Students encounter the formal term 'variable' only after the concrete distinction is secure, making the Math_Vault formalisation in the parent module feel like a natural naming of something already understood rather than a new abstract concept.
On slide 23, replace 'Such letters that are used to represent numbers we don't know yet are called VARIABLES.' with 'Such letters that stand for a number we don't know yet are called special letters in mathematics — we will give them their proper name soon.' Reserve the formal term 'variable' for the slide 32 summary panel only, where the blueprint permits the forward pointer.
Highest priority: (1) Add explicit dev notes to screens 3, 6, 11, and 12 specifying animation outcomes, state transitions, and timing (gates G5 and G7). (2) Insert a Commit-Before-Reveal classification screen after slide 15 where students independently classify the plate and sack as fixed or changing before labels are revealed (P2, D4). (3) Add a fourth MCQ option on screen 13 ('a — because a stands for apple the fruit') with Counterexample Feedback to surface and correct the M252 concrete-phase misconception (X2). (4) Insert a Match Pairs drag-to-classify screen between screens 14 and 15 for independent application of the fixed/changing distinction (D2, X1).
Path to 100 — Priority Actions ⊘ Must fix 6 · ↑ Should fix 20 · 2 mastery gaps
⊘ Must fix G5 State machine readable
Add explicit dev notes to screens 3, 6, 11, and 12. For screen 3: specify the sack-tap animation (e.g., 'on tap: apples animate out of sack onto plate; plate populates with N apples; advance to slide 4 after animation completes'). For screen 6: specify what the » tap triggers (e.g., 'on tap: transition to slide 13 which shows the side-by-side two-column layout'). For screen 11: specify the sack-tap outcome (e.g., 'on tap: advance to screen 12 / slide 18'). For screen 12: resolve the reveal_mode_uncertain flag — specify whether each frame is auto-advance (and the timer duration in ms) or tap-to-advance, and add this as a dev note on slide 18.
Developer can build the applet from the storyboard alone without calling the designer. All interactive screens have sufficient dev notes or slide-sequence information to implement the interaction and its outcome states.
⊘ Must fix G7 Screen zones present
Add a dev notes box (or inline annotation) to slides 3, 12, 17, and 18 specifying: (slide 3) the sack-tap animation spec and advance trigger; (slide 12) what the » tap transitions to; (slide 17) the sack-tap outcome; (slide 18) the frame-advance mechanism (auto-timer ms or tap) for the animation sequence through slide 23. This resolves the missing third zone on all four affected interactive screens.
Every interactive screen has all three layers: action instruction + scaffolding text + dev notes. Non-interactive summary and expository screens are exempt.
⊘ Must fix✎editmedium confidence AP4 Click-to-reveal
For screen 12: Remove the explicit tap affordance on slide 22 (the intermediate 'Tap » to understand what these letters are called' frame) and let it auto-advance to slide 23 where the VARIABLES definition appears. The definition reveal on slide 23 is the cognitively meaningful moment and can retain its tap affordance. For screen 6: Change 'Tap » to compare the 2 quantities…' to an auto-advance transition so the side-by-side comparison appears without requiring a navigation click.
Screen 12 (slides 18–23): Thumbnails confirm that slides 18–21 auto-advance (only « and » navigation arrows visible, no explicit tap affordance on intermediate frames). However, slide 22 shows a highlighted finger-cursor icon on the » button with the explicit label 'Tap » to understand what these letters are called', and slide 23 shows 'Tap » to explore variables more' with the same highlighted cursor. These two final frames of the animation run require an explicit tap to advance, and the revealed content (the word VARIABLES on slide 23) is the key vocabulary payoff — this is cognitively non-trivial and justified. However, slide 22's tap-to-advance reveals only the transition to slide 23 (which itself requires another tap), making slide 22's tap a pure navigation click with no new content revealed on that frame itself. This is a marginal click-to-reveal instance. Screen 6 (slide 12): 'Tap » to compare the 2 quantities…' advances to slide 13 which shows the same two quantities side-by-side with labels — the comparison layout could simply be the next auto-advance frame rather than requiring an explicit tap.
⊘ Must fix✎edithigh confidence AP5 Feedback inflation
Replace 'Yay!' praise with neutral confirmatory feedback that redirects attention to the concept: e.g., slide 9 → 'Correct — 3 apples. Now look at the sack: can you count those?'; slide 27 → 'Right — 'a' stands for the number of apples. Notice: we chose 'a' because it reminds us of apples, but any letter would work.'; slide 31 → 'Good — since 'a' already represents apples, we need a different letter for oranges. 'b' or 'x' both work.' Reserve celebratory feedback for the final consolidation screen (slide 32) where the full concept has been assembled.
Slide 9: 'Yay! That's 3 countable apples on the plate…' — awarded after counting 3 visible apples on a plate, which is a trivially easy perceptual task requiring no algebraic reasoning. Slide 27: 'That's right! The variable 'a' is the number of apples in the sack!' — awarded for selecting 'a' from a 3-option MCQ where the variable label 'a' is already displayed on the sack image, making the correct answer visually obvious. Slide 31: 'Yay! We don't know that there are the same number of apples and oranges! Any letter except 'a' can be used for number of oranges!' — awarded for selecting any letter other than 'a', which is a low-demand exclusion task. None of these represent earned mastery of the variable concept.
⊘ Must fix✎editmedium confidence AP7 Decorative interaction
Screen 3: Replace the 'tap to pull out apples' mechanic with a brief auto-animation (apples appear from sack automatically) so the student's first deliberate interaction is the cognitively meaningful counting task on screen 4. Alternatively, add a reasoning prompt to the tap: 'Tap the sack — do you think you can count what's inside?' to make the action conceptually purposeful. Screen 6: Convert the 'Tap » to compare' to an auto-advance so the comparison layout appears without a navigation click, freeing the student's attention for the conceptual contrast on the next screen.
Screen 3: 'Tap the sack to pull out some apples' — the tap action reveals apples from the sack. This is a purely motivational animation; the student learns nothing from the tap itself that they would not learn from simply seeing the apples already on the plate. The interaction does not encode a misconception or require reasoning. Screen 6: 'Tap » to compare the 2 quantities…' — tapping advances to a side-by-side layout. The comparison is the pedagogical content, but the tap itself is decorative navigation rather than a reasoning action. The MCQ distractors on screen 5 (slide 10: '100', '50', 'We don't know') are well-chosen — '100' and '50' encode the misconception that a specific number can be guessed, so screen 5's interaction is NOT decorative. Screen 13's distractors ('3', 'a', 'x') are also meaningful — '3' encodes confusing the known quantity with the variable, 'x' encodes using a different letter. Screen 13 is NOT decorative.
⊘ Must fix✎edithigh confidence AP9 Action without reasoning
Screen 11 (slide 17): Add a one-sentence reasoning prompt above or below the action instruction, e.g., 'Think: can you count what's inside without opening it?' before 'Tap the Sack to explore unknown quantities.' Screen 3 (slide 4): Add a reasoning prompt to the plate-tap instruction: 'Look carefully — how many can you count?' to orient the student's attention before they act.
Screen 11 (slide 17): 'Tap the Sack to explore unknown quantities' — the action instruction is present but there is no scaffolding text guiding what the student should think about before tapping. The student is told to act but not what to notice or reason about. Screen 3 (slides 3–4): 'Tap the sack to pull out some apples' / 'Tap the plate to count the apples on it' — pure procedural instructions with no reasoning prompt (e.g., 'What do you think is inside?'). By contrast, screen 5 (slide 10) has both an action ('Tap the correct answer') and an implicit reasoning scaffold ('How many apples do you think are in the sack?' with the '?' visual), so screen 5 is acceptable.
+1
◦ Consider P2 P · 4/5 +inserthigh confidence
After screen 6 (student confirms 'We don't know' for the sack), insert a Commit-Before-Reveal classification screen: present both the plate and the sack side by side with two tap-buttons beneath each — 'Fixed quantity' and 'Changing quantity'.
+1
◦ Consider P3 P · 3/4 ✎editmedium confidence
On screen 12 (the animation sequence slides 18–23), after the letter 'a' settles on the sack, add a one-line callout: 'In mathematics, we write this as: a apples — where a is the number of apples in the sack.' This makes the concrete-to-abstract transition explicit and mirrors the textbook's p.129 framing ('a is the variable; it tells us how many').
+1
◦ Consider P6 P · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On screen 12 (slides 19–22), promote the 'Remember — a is THE NUMBER of apples, not the fruit itself' note from a secondary callout to a prominent student-facing callout box (e.g., a highlighted banner beneath the sack image).
+1
◦ Consider D1 D · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
Convert at least one of the passive observe screens (screens 8–10, slides 14–16) into a Commit-Before-Reveal moment: after the plate label 'known quantities' appears (slide 15), add a tap-to-classify prompt for the sack before the 'unknown quantities' label is revealed (slide 17).
+1
◦ Consider D2 D · 3/4 +inserthigh confidence
After screen 14 (oranges variable selection), insert a Match Pairs screen where students drag three quantity description cards ('3 apples on the plate', 'apples in Candra's sack (a)', 'oranges in Candra's father's sack') to Fixed or Changing columns without prompts.
+1
◦ Consider D3 D · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
Remove the dev-note banner ('Slide numbers 18–22 are same screen — on which transitions happen one after another') from the student-facing layer of slides 18–22.
+1
◦ Consider D4 D · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
Convert screens 9–10 (slides 15–16, the 'known quantities' / 'unknown quantities' label reveal) into a Commit-Before-Reveal interaction: after the plate label appears, ask the student to tap whether the sack quantity is 'known' or 'unknown' before the label is revealed.
+1
◦ Consider D5 D · 2/3 +inserthigh confidence
Between screen 12 (slide 23, 'VARIABLES' definition) and screen 13 (slide 24, variable-identification question), insert a one-screen consolidation moment: display the sack with 'a apples' locked in (the final state of the animation) and a brief confirmatory statement — 'So we use the letter a to represent how many apples are in the sack.
+1
◦ Consider D6 D · 2/3 ✎editmedium confidence
For screen 12 (slides 18–22): confirm in the dev note that each letter substitution (? → x → y → z → a) is student-paced (tap » to advance), not auto-advancing.
+1
◦ Consider D7 D · 3/4 ✎editmedium confidence
On slide 32 (summary screen), add an explicit forward pointer after the 'variables' definition: 'In the next activity, we will learn the proper mathematical names for these ideas.' This bridges the applet's concrete-phase language ('known/unknown quantities') to the module's Math_Vault formalisation of 'variable' and 'constant', as specified in the blueprint's Stage 6 Consolidate convention requirement.
+1
◦ Consider X1 X · 3/4 +inserthigh confidence
Add a drag-to-classify screen between screen 13 (variable identification MCQ) and screen 14 (oranges scenario) using the Match Pairs IAS archetype.
+1
◦ Consider X2 X · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On screen 13 (slides 24–27), add a fourth MCQ option: 'a — because a stands for apple (the fruit)' as the M252 distractor.
+1
◦ Consider X4 X · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
On screen 12 (slides 18–22), add dev-note timing annotations: specify 1000–1500ms per letter transition (x→y→z→a) so the student can read each 'Remember' callout before the next letter appears.
+1
◦ Consider X5 X · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
Add fingerTap cue GIF declarations in the dev notes for: (a) slide 3 (tap the sack), (b) slide 17 (tap the sack to explore unknown quantities), and (c) slide 28 (tap the sack of oranges).
+1
◦ Consider X6 X · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
Expand dev notes for screen 12 (slides 18–22) to specify: transition timing per letter (recommended 1000–1500ms), whether student taps to advance or auto-advances, and the exact state of the sack label at each frame.
+1
◦ Consider X7 X · 1/2 ✎edithigh confidence
Add audio cue specifications to the dev notes for all interactive screens: (a) correct tap/numpad entry → rising two-tone chime (e.g., C4→E4, ~300ms); (b) incorrect tap → descending two-tone tone (e.g., E4→C4, ~300ms); (c) 'Activity Completed!' screen → celebratory ascending arpeggio (3–4 notes, ~800ms).
+1
◦ Consider L1 L · 3/4 ✎edithigh confidence
On slide 23, replace 'Such letters that are used to represent numbers we don't know yet are called VARIABLES.' with 'Such letters that stand for a number we don't know yet are called special letters in mathematics — we will give them their proper name soon.' Reserve the formal term 'variable' for the Stage 6 (slide 32) summary panel only, where the blueprint permits the forward pointer.
+1
◦ Consider L3 L · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
On slides 14–16 (screens 8–10), add a reasoning prompt beneath the side-by-side contrast: 'Look at the plate and the sack.
+1
◦ Consider L5 L · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
Add dev notes to slides 5–9 (numpad screen), slides 10–11 (three-option tap), slides 24–27 (three-option tap), and slides 29–31 (three-option tap) specifying: minimum touch target size 44×44px per WCAG 2.1 SC 2.5.5; numpad keys minimum 48×48px with 8px gap between keys; » and « navigation arrows minimum 44×44px tap area.
+1
◦ Consider L6 L · 2/3 ✎edithigh confidence
Add dev notes to slides 3–4, 10–12, 28–31 (all slides with PICTURE elements depicting sacks, plates, apples, oranges) specifying alt-text for each image: e.g., slide 3 sack image: alt='A tied cloth sack containing an unknown number of apples'; slide 4 plate image: alt='A plate with 3 red apples on it'.
Content role not fulfilled.
Interaction progression incomplete.
Translation Notes (21) — localisation metadata, not scored

The scorers encountered these bilingual term references in the curriculum context and blueprint. They are preserved here as deliverables for a later translation or localisation pass. The native-language fragments were NOT used to score vocabulary coverage — only the primary term was matched against delivered slide content.

Primary Native Lang Slide Type Context
variable variabel Indonesian — vocabulary Core algebraic term; Indonesian form is a direct cognate of the English. Not yet introduced as a formal term in this app
constant konstanta Indonesian — vocabulary A fixed numerical value in an algebraic expression with no variable; Indonesian form is a direct cognate. Not yet introd
algebraic form bentuk aljabar Indonesian — vocabulary A mathematical phrase combining variables, coefficients, and constants; Indonesian uses 'bentuk aljabar' (literally 'alg
coefficient koefisien Indonesian — vocabulary The numerical multiplier of a variable in an algebraic term; Indonesian form is a direct cognate. Out of scope for this
term suku Indonesian — vocabulary A single component of an algebraic expression separated by + or −; Indonesian uses 'suku' (literally 'part/member'), not
n n Indonesian — notation The letter used as a variable to represent the pattern number; notation is identical in both languages. Out of scope for
3n 3n Indonesian — notation Shorthand for 3 × n; the multiplication sign is omitted when a number multiplies a variable. Out of scope for this apple
algebraic expression bentuk aljabar Indonesian — vocabulary Synonym for 'algebraic form' used in the chapter; Indonesian uses the same term 'bentuk aljabar' for both. Out of scope
fixed quantity besaran tetap Indonesian — convention Everyday-language label used in this applet for a quantity whose value is known and does not change (e.g., 3 apples on t
changing quantity besaran yang berubah Indonesian — convention Everyday-language label used in this applet for a quantity whose value is not known and may vary (e.g., apples in the sa
substitution substitusi Indonesian — vocabulary Replacing a variable with a specific numerical value to evaluate an expression; Indonesian form is a direct cognate. Out
like terms suku sejenis Indonesian — vocabulary Terms with identical variable parts; Indonesian uses 'suku sejenis'. Out of scope for this applet — carried forward for
unlike terms suku tidak sejenis Indonesian — vocabulary Terms with different variable parts; Indonesian uses 'suku tidak sejenis'. Out of scope for this applet — carried forwar
collect like terms menyederhanakan suku sejenis Indonesian — convention The process of combining like terms to simplify an expression; Indonesian textbooks typically use 'menyederhanakan'. Out
distributive property sifat distributif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that a(b + c) = ab + ac; Indonesian uses 'sifat distributif'. Out of scope for this applet — carried forwar
commutative property sifat komutatif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that a + b = b + a; Indonesian uses 'sifat komutatif'. Out of scope for this applet — carried forward for d
associative property sifat asosiatif Indonesian — vocabulary The property that (a + b) + c = a + (b + c); Indonesian uses 'sifat asosiatif'. Out of scope for this applet — carried f
equivalence kesetaraan Indonesian — vocabulary The state of two algebraic expressions having the same value for all variable values; Indonesian uses 'kesetaraan' or 'e
expand menjabarkan Indonesian — vocabulary To apply the distributive property to remove brackets; Indonesian uses 'menjabarkan'. Out of scope for this applet — car
factor memfaktorkan Indonesian — vocabulary To rewrite an expression as a product of its factors; Indonesian uses 'memfaktorkan'. Out of scope for this applet — car
degree derajat Indonesian — vocabulary The highest exponent of the variable in a polynomial; Indonesian uses 'derajat', which also means angle degree and acade
Research Brief
Learning Objective
Identify a changing quantity in a concrete everyday context (apples on a plate) and distinguish it from a fixed quantity, as the first step toward representing changing quantities with a letter (variable).
Misconceptions (3)
#252 — At this applet's concrete scope, the M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252
Misconception medium
At this applet's concrete scope, the M252 precursor is the tendency to treat the letter 'a' (for apples in the sack) as a name for the fruit rather than a count of the fruit — i.e., 'a means apple, not how many apples.' The module trace confirms this is explicitly addressed at Slide 8 with the callout 'The letter a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not a name for the apples.' This applet is the source of that correction. The deeper M252 form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for) is not yet activatable at this applet's scope because no algebraic notation or equation is present.
Within the applet interaction: after the student assigns the letter 'a' to the sack of apples, present a distractor choice — 'Does a mean: (A) the apple fruit, or (B) how many apples are in the sack?' If the student selects (A), deploy Counterexample Feedback: 'a is not the name of the fruit — we already know it's an apple! a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack.' This is the concrete-phase surface of M252.
.
Severity: medium none IAS: Counterexample Feedback
#251 — M251
Misconception high
M251 is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not introduce algebraic expressions, coefficients, or the notation 3n — it operates entirely at the concrete level of distinguishing a known quantity (3 apples on the plate) from an unknown quantity (apples in the sack). The order-of-operations error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) and the precursor form (combining 1 + 3n into 4n) both require algebraic notation that is absent here. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M251 (and its precursor form) is activated only when students encounter the expression 1 + 3n, which is introduced later in the module sequence. Monitor in the module's Math_Trap slides and in sibling applets G7C4M1A1 and G7C4M1A3.
 is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not introduce algebraic expressions, coefficients, or the notation 3n — it operates entirely at the concrete level of distinguishing a known quantity (3 apples on the plate) from an unknown quantity (apples in the sack). The order-of-operations error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) and the precursor form (combining 1 + 3n into 4n) both require algebraic notation that is absent here. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M251
Misconception high
M251 is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not introduce algebraic expressions, coefficients, or the notation 3n — it operates entirely at the concrete level of distinguishing a known quantity (3 apples on the plate) from an unknown quantity (apples in the sack). The order-of-operations error (treating 2 + 3x as 5x) and the precursor form (combining 1 + 3n into 4n) both require algebraic notation that is absent here. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M251 (and its precursor form) is activated only when students encounter the expression 1 + 3n, which is introduced later in the module sequence. Monitor in the module's Math_Trap slides and in sibling applets G7C4M1A1 and G7C4M1A3.
 (and its precursor form) is activated only when students encounter the expression 1 + 3n, which is introduced later in the module sequence. Monitor in the module's Math_Trap slides and in sibling applets G7C4M1A1 and G7C4M1A3.
Severity: high none IAS: none
#250 — M250
Misconception medium
M250 (guess-and-check as the only equation-solving strategy) is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not ask students to solve equations. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M250 is activated only when students are asked to solve equations (e.g., 3x + 7 = 28). Monitor in later equation-focused modules.
 (guess-and-check as the only equation-solving strategy) is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not ask students to solve equations. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M250
Misconception medium
M250 (guess-and-check as the only equation-solving strategy) is not activatable at this applet's scope. The applet does not ask students to solve equations. Included for completeness per checklist requirement; no instructional action in this applet.
Not applicable at this applet's scope. M250 is activated only when students are asked to solve equations (e.g., 3x + 7 = 28). Monitor in later equation-focused modules.
 is activated only when students are asked to solve equations (e.g., 3x + 7 = 28). Monitor in later equation-focused modules.
Severity: medium none IAS: none
Visual Models (1)
VIR 161 — Variable as Pattern Continuation
Primary visual anchor for this applet. Ground the distinction between a fixed quantity (3 apples on the plate — countable, visible, known) and a changing/unknown quantity (apples in the sack — not countable, not visible, unknown). The fruit-sack scenario is the concrete instantiation of VIR 161's core insight: a letter is not a mystery to solve but a label for 'how many' — a quantity that could be any number. Position at the opening moment of the applet, before any letter is introduced, so students perceive the fixed/changing distinction in the physical scenario first.
concrete introduce_concept moderate
IAS Patterns (5)
Progressive Reveal
Actions: Attend, Perceive Structure
When: Use to introduce the two quantities sequentially: first show the plate with 3 apples (fixed, countable), then reveal the sack (unknown, not countable). The sequential reveal forces students to perceive the contrast before they are asked to classify. This is the concrete-phase entry into the fixed/changing distinction.
Side-by-Side Contrast
Actions: Compare, Map Representations, Perceive Structure
When: Use to display the plate (known quantity = 3) alongside the sack (unknown quantity = a) after the reveal, so students can directly compare the two types of quantity. The two-column known-vs-unknown layout from the module trace (Slide 8) is the correct realisation of this archetype at concrete phase.
Counterexample Feedback
Actions: Demonstrate a case that breaks a false rule the learner proposed
When: Deploy when a student selects the distractor 'a means the apple fruit' — show that the fruit already has a name ('apple') and that the letter a must therefore mean something else: how many. This is the concrete-phase surface of M252 and should fire as immediate corrective feedback within the applet interaction.
Commit-Before-Reveal
Actions: Predict, Commit
When: Use at the classification moment: ask the student to tap/select whether the number of apples on the plate is a 'fixed quantity' or a 'changing quantity' before the answer is confirmed. Locks in the student's reasoning and increases engagement with the distinction. Repeat for the sack quantity.
Match Pairs
Actions: Map Representations, Compare, Classify
When: Use as the applet's consolidation interaction: present two or three quantity descriptions (e.g., '3 apples on the plate', 'apples in the sack', 'oranges in Candra's father's sack') and ask students to drag each to the correct column (Fixed / Changing). This is the Shout_Out_Loud moment in the applet and directly prepares students for the module's variable/constant vocabulary formalisation.
Convention Flags (4)
Letter 'a' as quantity label vs. fruit name
Introduce the letter 'a' only after the sack scenario is established and the question 'how many apples are in the sack?' has been posed. Frame the letter explicitly as answering the question 'how many?' — not as a name. Use the callout: 'a tells us HOW MANY apples are in the sack — it is not the name of the fruit.'
Risk: When students first see 'a apples', they may read 'a' as an abbreviation for 'apple' (the fruit) rather than as a label for the count of apples. This is the concrete-phase surface of M252 and is the single most important convention to establish in this applet. The module trace confirms this risk is real — the Slide 8 callout was specifically designed to address it.
Fixed quantity vs. constant — vocabulary sequencing
Use only 'fixed quantity' and 'changing quantity' (or 'known quantity' and 'unknown quantity') as the student-facing labels in this applet. Reserve 'constant' and 'variable' for the module's Math_Vault slide that follows this applet in the sequence. The applet's summary slide (Slide 31 in the topic) uses 'b means THE NUMBER of oranges' — this phrasing is correct and should be preserved.
Risk: The applet operates at the concrete phase and uses the everyday language 'fixed quantity' and 'changing quantity'. The formal vocabulary 'constant' and 'variable' is introduced in the module's Math_Vault (Slide 9 in the module trace). If the applet introduces 'constant' and 'variable' prematurely, it front-loads abstract vocabulary before the concrete distinction is secure.
Letter choice: 'a' for apples, 'b' for oranges
After establishing 'a' for the apple sack, explicitly demonstrate that any letter (not just 'a') could have been used — the choice of letter is arbitrary. When introducing 'b' for oranges, state: 'We use b here, but we could use any letter — as long as it is different from a, because a and b represent different quantities.' This directly addresses the abbreviation misconception.
Risk: Using 'a' for apples and 'b' for oranges risks reinforcing the misconception that variable letters are abbreviations for the object's name (a = apple, b = banana/orange). The module trace confirms this risk and addresses it with the 'HOW MANY' callout. The applet's summary (Slide 31) correctly states 'Any letter except a can be used for number of oranges' — this is the right message but must be delivered carefully.
Shout_Out_Loud interaction — oranges in the sack
Sequence the Shout_Out_Loud after the fixed/changing classification interaction is complete. The question 'How many oranges?' should feel like a natural extension of the established pattern: 'We couldn't count the apples in the sack, and we can't count the oranges either — so we use a different letter, b, to represent how many oranges there are.'
Risk: The applet summary (Slide 31) shows a Shout_Out_Loud template asking 'How many oranges are in the sack Candra's father brought?' with the answer 'We don't know — that's why we use a letter!' This is the correct concrete framing. The risk is that the Shout_Out_Loud fires before the fixed/changing distinction is secure, making the letter introduction feel arbitrary.
Research Gaps (3)
visual_model: No KB VIR exists for the two-column fixed-vs-changing quantity table that is the primary concrete scaffold for this applet. VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the closest KB record but describes a growing pattern context, not the static fruit-sack scenario used here. The applet's visual model is novel (confirmed by the module trace as 'scorer_observed_novel') and has no KB evidence anchor.
Medium. The applet's core visual is well-designed (confirmed by the module trace) but lacks KB evidence grounding. Blueprint builders must design this without a VIR anchor. Recommend KB insertion of a VIR for 'Fixed vs. Changing Quantity — Concrete Object Contrast' at concrete CPA phase.
interactive: No KB IAS entry exists for a 'classify-and-label' interactive at the concrete phase — where students observe two physical objects (plate vs. sack), classify each as fixed or changing, and then assign a letter to the changing quantity. IAS 21 (Match Pairs) is the closest available archetype and is recommended, but a dedicated 'quantity classification' archetype would better capture the pedagogical intent of this applet.
Low-medium. The Match Pairs archetype is sufficient for the consolidation interaction, but the opening classification moment (Commit-Before-Reveal on fixed vs. changing) lacks a formally evidenced IAS archetype. The Progressive Reveal + Commit-Before-Reveal combination is recommended as the best available fit.
misconception: The concrete-phase surface of M252 — 'a means the apple fruit, not how many apples' — is not explicitly described in the KB misconception record for M252. The KB record describes the deeper form (variable as mystery unknown to solve for), not the letter-as-abbreviation confusion that arises at the concrete phase when letters are first introduced alongside physical objects.
Medium. The applet's Counterexample Feedback for this surface form must be designed from first principles. The module trace confirms the 'HOW MANY' callout is effective at the surface level, but the KB does not provide a formally evidenced surfacing strategy for this concrete-phase variant. Recommend KB refinement of M252 to include the letter-as-abbreviation precursor.
Synthesis Narrative
This applet is the module's concrete-phase entry point — the moment when students first encounter the idea that some quantities can be counted (fixed) and some cannot (changing/unknown), and that a letter can stand in for the count of the latter. Its instructional job is narrow and precise: establish the perceptual distinction between a fixed quantity and a changing quantity using a familiar Indonesian everyday context (Candra's fruit scenario), and introduce the letter 'a' as a label for 'how many apples are in the sack' — not as a name for the fruit. Everything that follows in the module (the Math_Vault formalisation of 'variable' and 'constant', the Math_Trap for M252, the notation 3n) depends on this perceptual foundation being secure. The recommended interaction sequence flows directly from the KB evidence. VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the primary visual anchor, adapted here to the static fruit-sack scenario rather than a growing pattern: the plate of 3 apples is the 'fixed' element (like the constant in a pattern), and the sack of unknown apples is the 'changing' element (like the variable). The Progressive Reveal archetype establishes the contrast sequentially — plate first, then sack — before any classification is demanded. The Side-by-Side Contrast archetype then holds both quantities in view simultaneously, enabling the Commit-Before-Reveal classification interaction: students tap to identify which quantity is fixed and which is changing before the answer is confirmed. This sequence mirrors the Exploration 4.1 table structure from the textbook (p.127) at the most concrete level, before any table or notation is introduced. The applet's single most important convention risk is the letter-as-abbreviation confusion: students seeing 'a apples' may read 'a' as shorthand for 'apple' rather than as a count. The Counterexample Feedback archetype is the correct response mechanism — 'We already know it's an apple; a tells us HOW MANY.' The Shout_Out_Loud moment (oranges in Candra's father's sack) extends the pattern to a second unknown quantity and introduces 'b', reinforcing that letter choice is arbitrary and that different unknowns need different letters. This is the applet's consolidation moment and directly prepares students for the module's Math_Vault formalisation of 'variable'. M251 and M250 are not activatable at this applet's scope and require no instructional action here; the parent module's Math_Trap slides are the correct venue for those misconceptions once algebraic notation is introduced.
Inheritance Notes
Module research confirmed: (1) VIR 161 (Variable as Pattern Continuation) is the chapter's recommended concrete-phase entry point for the variable concept — this applet is the module's first concrete-phase touchpoint and should deploy VIR 161 as its primary visual anchor. (2) VIR 203 (Function Machines) is confirmed as the CPA bridge from concrete pattern to abstract notation — not yet needed at this applet's scope, which is purely concrete. (3) The implicit multiplication convention flag (3 × n = 3n) is the module's highest-priority notation shift — not yet in scope for this applet, which precedes notation introduction. (4) M252 (variable as mystery unknown) is the module's primary misconception target — this applet's concrete framing of 'changing vs. fixed quantity' is the prerequisite scaffold for the M252 Math_Trap that will fire later in the module. (5) The fixed-vs-changing quantity table gap (no KB VIR) was confirmed at module scope — this applet's two-column known-vs-unknown layout is the concrete realisation of that gap. (6) The module knowledge trace confirms that the parent module (G7C4M1) already establishes 'variable' and 'constant' via the fruit-sack scenario (Candra's apples/oranges) — this applet IS that concrete scenario and is the source of those established concepts. (7) The module trace confirms that M252 and M251 are NOT yet surfaced in the module as reviewed — this applet's interaction design must lay the perceptual groundwork (changing vs. fixed) that makes the later Math_Trap slides coherent. (8) All translation notes from module research are carried forward in full.
Ideal Blueprint
Blueprint LO
Identify a changing quantity in a concrete everyday context (apples on a plate vs. apples in a sack) and distinguish it from a fixed quantity, as the first step toward representing changing quantities with a letter (variable).

How to read this review

A review is the scorer's diagnosis of a single module (or applet). The per-slide grid at the bottom is the main working area; the sections above it are summaries and context.

Header & scores

  • Verdict (ship / upgrade / rework / fail) and overall score summarise the compiled review.
  • Mastery coverage is the S8 estimate of how much of the LO the module actually delivers.
  • Reviewer directives banner (amber, near the top) lists any approved-gate directives that flowed into the scorers this run.
  • Quadrant strip shows P / D / X / L percent scores with S9 blurbs.

Vocabulary Registry

A module-level table of every term this module is responsible for: A1's planned terms (with authoritative definitions), KB-supplementary terms, and scorer-discovered terms. Each row shows delivery status (established / mentioned / not introduced) and source. This is the one-glance answer to "what vocabulary does this module own?"

What's Working Well

Positive observations surfaced from scorer reasoning — criteria that scored at max, grouped by quadrant. The full reasoning text is included so you can see what the scorer thought was strong and why. Previously these stayed buried inside the collapsed Scorer details; this card pulls them up front.

Gates & Antipatterns

Module-level scoring signals. Gates are hard/critical pass/fail checks; antipatterns are structural issues found by S7. Click any failed gate or antipattern id for a description in the modal.

Slide Review (per-slide grid)

Each slide row has the thumbnail, the scorer-synthesised slide action wrapped in a priority chip (see symbol legend below), and a collapsed "Scorer details" block with the full criterion annotations. Below that sits the enrichment strip — each row is a distinct commitment the blueprint or scorer anchored to this slide:

  • Concept — what the reviewer actually saw (or thinks should be added) on this slide. Chips are tagged by action: introduces (green — slide establishes the concept for the first time in the module), reinforces / practices (blue — slide revisits a concept established earlier), add here (red dashed — reviewer recommends the slide deliver this concept but it currently does not), reinforce harder (amber dashed — slide mentions the concept but the reviewer recommends exercising it harder). Each chip carries a strength tag (explicit / partial / weak / missing) and an evidence snippet in the tooltip. Sourced from the S6 scorer's concepts_on_slide array (P and D quadrants) — what the reviewer observed, not what the blueprint planned.
  • Vocab introduced — A1-planned terms the module introduces at this slide, from blueprint.vocabulary_introduction_plan[].introduced_at_slide. Chip tooltip shows the authoritative definition.
  • Vocab in screen (applet reviews only) — terms the screen exercises. Sub-tag reinforced means the screen actively practices the term (drag / tap / input / generate); used means the term only appears passively.
  • Misc addressed — misconception ids the slide explicitly targets, with effectiveness 0/3. Primary source is blueprint.slides[].misconceptions_targeted (plan); the scorer's evidence_slides feeds the effectiveness score. Click the chip for the full how-addressed narrative.
  • Visual model — VIR id + title + CPA phase used on this slide. Click to open the VIR's full SVG preview and KB metadata in a modal.
  • Prerequisites — prior-module / prior-chapter concepts this slide reactivates. Populated on Activate-section bridges. From blueprint.slides[].prerequisites_activated.
  • Convention — false-friend or convention requirements the slide must satisfy. From blueprint.convention_requirements[].addressed_at_slide.
  • Textbook — textbook pages the slide uses, with usage tag (use_as_is / adapted / supplemented / referenced). From blueprint.textbook_alignment[].slide_positions.
  • Exit — marker on the slide that is the module's exit-verification question (from blueprint.trope_map.exit_question).
  • Flagged — criterion ids the scorer rated below max on this slide. Use this to jump directly to the problem spots.
  • Stage (applet reviews only) — 1-6 stage tag + stage name + cognitive action + interaction mode + scaffolding level + CPA phase, derived from the applet blueprint screen that this PPTX slide belongs to.

Click any chip with a tooltip indicator to open its full detail in a modal.

Priority & operation symbols

The Path to 100 section and each slide's action card use pill chips instead of inline text. Scan by glyph:

  • ⊘ Must fix — gate or antipattern requires this before ship
  • ↑ Should fix — quality upgrade recommended
  • ◦ Consider — nice-to-have, not blocking

Operation chips next to each action name the recommendation verb from the recommendation protocol:

  • ✖delete remove slide / content
  • +insert add new slide / element
  • ✎edit modify existing content
  • ↵merge combine two into one
  • ✂split one slide into several
  • ⇄relocate move to different position — notably for worked-example → applet conversion

Mastery Assessment

S8's narrative assessment: coverage percent with notes, what students CAN do after the module, fluency gaps, conceptual depth, transfer readiness, and the mastery path to 100. Per-misconception detail now lives in each slide's enrichment strip instead of a separate list here.

Path to 100

Priority-ordered upgrade actions from the scorer's upgrade_map, grouped as:

  • Gate recommendations — must-fix items from failed hard/critical gates or antipatterns. Lead with a priority chip + operation chip + source label.
  • Priority actions — per-criterion upgrades sorted by gap magnitude. Priority is derived from the gap (gap ≥ 3 → must-fix, ≥ 2 → should-fix, otherwise consider) when the scorer didn't emit one explicitly. Each row links back to the criterion's annotation anchor in the slide grid.
  • Mastery gaps — narrative gaps from S8 that don't have a specific criterion owner.

Research brief & Blueprint overlays

The linked Research brief and Blueprint overlays contain the upstream inputs the scorers used — misconceptions, VIRs, IAS patterns, slide sequence, vocabulary introduction plan, textbook alignment. Open them from the overlay buttons to cross-reference with the review.
